DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Aug-16-2019 Overruled
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Max G. Carpinelli, Esq. of Wilson Elser Moskowitz Edelman & Dicker LLP, T:408.562.3672, for defendant & moving party;
Alik Ourfalian, Esq. of Lawyers for Justice, T:818.265.1020, for plaintiff & opposing party.

RULING: The tentative ruling was contested and argued. At the conclusion of arguments, the Court adopted the tentative ruling on DEFENDANT HEALTH ADVOCATES LLC'S DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT as follows:

Health Advocates, LLC's demurrer is overruled in its entirety. Plaintiff no longer simply parrots the language of the Labor Code but pleads specific factual examples regarding how defendant systematically violated the Labor Code. (Second Amended Complaint Paras. 16, 33, 38, 39, 61.) Plaintiff pleads facts showing an ascertainable and sufficiently numerous class and a well-defined community of interest. (See Tarkington v. California Unemployment Ins. Appeals Bd. (2009) 172 Cal.App.4th 1494, 1511 [recognizing that resolution of class issues on the pleadings is apt to occur in a mass tort action rather than a wage and hour action].) The court further notes that a class action is not inappropriate merely because each member of the class may at some point be required to make an individual showing as to his or her eligibility to recover. (See Bell v. Farmers Ins. Exchange (2004) 115 Cal.App.4th 715, 742.)

Order signed in open court.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer, Jr.; Clerk: Gina Gonzales; Court Reporter: Maria A. Torreano CSR#8600, email: maria.torreano@gmail.com

Max G. Carpinelli, Esq., Wilson Elser Moskowitz Edelman, Telephone 213-443-5100, appearing for Defendant Heatlh Advocates, LLC; (CourtCall)

RULING - A R G U E D. Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

The Court adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

DEFENDANT HEALTH ADVOCATES, LLC's DEMURRER TO 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT.

Health Advocates, LLC's demurrer is SUSTAINED WITH 20 DAYS LEAVE TO AMEND.

The court grants Health Advocates' request for judicial notice of the pleadings and rulings in the federal and state wage and hour actions. The court finds that those documents have no probative value as they involve different actions and this court is solely concerned with whether plaintiff's first amended complaint in the instant action pleads sufficient facts to allege individual and/or class claims.

The demurrer to all nine causes of action is sustained with 20 days leave to amend. "[S]imply parroting the language of [the Labor Code] in the complaint is insufficient to state a cause of action under the statute." (Hawkins v. TACA Internat. Airlines, S.A. (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 466, 478.) Plaintiff's eighth cause of action seeks relief for unfair competition. "A plaintiff alleging unfair business practices under these statutes must state with reasonable particularity the facts supporting the statutory elements of the violation." (Khoury v. Maly's of California, Inc. (1993) 14 Cal.App.4th 612, 619.)

Plaintiff's first amended complaint pleads simple conclusions of law that do no properly advise Health Advocates of its alleged wrongdoing. There are no facts regarding plaintiff's or class members' employment experience with Health Advocates.

For this reason, the class allegations are also insufficient. Courts may resolve the issue of class treatment on demurrer. (See Tucker v. Pacific Bell Mobile Services (2012) 208 Cal.App.4th 201, 211.) The court recognizes that resolution of the class issue on the pleadings is apt to occur in a mass tort action rather than a wage and hour action. (See Tarkington v. California Unemployment Ins. Appeals Bd. (2009) 172 Cal.App.4th 1494 1511.) Nevertheless, plaintiff's current complaint is so vague that the court cannot properly conclude that class treatment is proper. Plaintiff has leave to plead facts showing an ascertainable and sufficiently numerous class and a well-defined community of interest. With regard to the community of interest requirement, plaintiff's amendments should demonstrate: (1) predominant common questions of law or fact; (2) class representatives with claims or defenses typical of the class; and (3) class representatives who can adequately represent the class. (See Ayala v. Antelope Valley Newspapers, Inc. (2014) 59 Cal.4th 522, 529-530.)

Order is signed.
